h1.title: How to Choose the Right PMS for Your Hotel? Step-by-Step Selection and Pre-Installation Guide
h1.intro: PMS (Property Management System) appears to be the operational heart of the hotel; but in reality, distribution (OTA/Channel Manager), revenue (Revenue Management), communication (Call Center), accounting/reporting and guest experience are built on this backbone. Therefore, it is necessary to decide from the perspective of "long-term integration and scale" rather than "short-term screen/habit".
quickSummary: For whom? Resort/city/boutique/villa hotel managers, sales-marketing and operations/IT ​​teams
What does it provide? Needs analysis → decision matrix → demo/pilot KPI plan → transition from selection to installation project
Why now? Choosing the wrong PMS increases the integration/operation burden in the future; The right choice carries the digital backbone for years.

ctaPrimary.label: Request PMS Selection & Installation Preliminary Analysis
ctaPrimary.text: Benefit: With a 30-45 minute preliminary meeting, we determine the needs according to your hotel type, clarify integration risks and correct demo KPIs.

sections.title: What is PMS and What Problem Does It Solve?

blocks.text: PMS basically enables the management of reservation-accommodation-invoicing-housekeeping-front office processes from a single center. However, the real gain in hotels is that the data becomes a single source of truth rather than a "single screen transaction": the right room stock, the right price/contract, the right guest profile and the right report output.
Consider the problems PMS solves under 4 headings:

items: Operations: check-in/out speed, room status accuracy, housekeeping coordination
Distribution: Stock/price synchronization with Channel Manager/OTA, reducing the risk of overbooking
Revenue & reporting: sales channel cost, cancellation/no-show trends, revenue reports (compliance with RMS)
Experience: guest notes/preferences, repeat stay signals, call center consistent information

blocks.text: ☑ Mini Check
“Do I expect PMS to be software or a backbone that standardizes business flow?”
What should I do?

items: Define PMS as a “data + integration backbone”, not a “front office screen”
Write down the 3 most critical flows: reservation → check-in → invoice; OTA → stock/price; report → decision
Who takes part in these 3 streams? (reception, revenue, sales, accounting, call center)

sections.title: PMS Needs Analysis by Hotel Type

blocks.text: The same PMS gives completely different results in different hotel types. Resort hotels on the Antalya–Belek–Side line; It stands out with its high volume, multiple outlets, peak season fluctuation and group/contract management. In the boutique/villa segment in Bodrum, fast sales, flexible price packages, different channel mix and "more work with fewer people" may be critical.
Perform needs analysis with the following 3 layers:

items: Must-have: room/inventory, check-in/out, invoice/folio, housekeeping, reports
Scale: multi-property/multi-outlet, group/contract, CRM basic integrations
Competition (Moat): Revenue Management compliance, call center flow, advanced reporting, automation

blocks.text: Decision Matrix (Hotel Type × Needs)
☑ Mini Check
“What is the most expensive mistake in my hotel type: overbooking, report blindness, or team time?”

When evaluating your PMS choice, consider the PMS + Channel Manager + OTA + Call Center + Revenue Management chain simultaneously; Because if these five are optimized separately, the total will be a disjointed system. For a framework approach on how to manage this chain: /tr/pms-ota-management and for integration details: /tr/pms-ota/ota-integration.
What should I do?

items: Clarify the hotel type (resort/city/boutique/villa) and create a 10-item “must-have” list
Write seasonality and channel mix (OTA share, direct share, call center share)
Identify 5 systems where “integration is a must” (OTA/CM, accounting, POS, RMS, call center)

blocks.label: Download PMS Selection Checklist
blocks.text: What does it do? Before the purchasing meeting, you see the deficiencies in 10 minutes and manage the demo/pilot process with the same checklist.

sections.title: What should I pay attention to when choosing PMS?

blocks.text: When choosing a PMS, first determine the needs according to your hotel type, then verify the modules and integrations (OTA/Channel Manager, accounting, call center, API). Measure the demo/pilot process with KPIs (speed, accuracy, report) and move on to the deployment project with a plan, clarifying the scope of data migration, support/SLA and integration in the contract.
Questions to Ask When Choosing a PMS (Question set)
The following set of questions breaks the “product demo” trap and leads to the actual purchase decision:
1) Operation questions

items: How many steps is the reception and housekeeping flow? Are there any unnecessary clicks?
Is the process blocked on a busy check-in day? (performance test)
Can user permissions be managed on a department basis?

blocks.text: 2) Distribution & channel questions (OTA/CM)

items: Is Channel Manager/OTA sync “instantaneous” or “periodic”? How is overbooking risk managed?
Is the cancel/no-show flow automatic or manual?

blocks.text: 3) Integration questions (critical part)

items: Which data flows in which direction between PMS–OTA–Call Center–Accounting–RMS?
Is there API integration or “off-the-shelf connector”? (technical note below)
How are integration errors reported, who intervenes?

blocks.text: 4) Report & decision questions

items: Are standard reports sufficient? How to make a special report if necessary?
Can channel cost and revenue analysis be made?

blocks.text: 5) Contract & risk questions

items: Scope of data migration and who is responsible?
What is the support model/SLA? How does emergency support work in season?
Is the exit plan (data export, end of contract) clear?

blocks.text: ☑ Mini Check
“Is what I'm buying software or a process + integration + support package?”
Cloud vs On-Premise (Classic) PMS: When is which?

items: Cloud PMS: Works over the Internet; Updates can be faster, access in multiple locations is easy.
Classic/On-premise PMS: Runs on the hotel's own server; There may be a control/habituation advantage in some facilities, but the maintenance and update burden may increase.

blocks.text: What should I do?

items: Reduce pre-demo question set to one page (use in purchasing meeting)
Talking about price without creating the integration map
Clarify the “data migration + support + integration scope” clauses in writing in the contract.

sections.title: Demo, Pilot and Reference Check

blocks.text: A demo is generally a “best case” presentation; The pilot is the test of the system in real life. So make the demo/pilot plan measurable.
Demo Plan (1–2 weeks): What should I measure?

items: Check-in/out scenario (busy day simulation)
OTA/Channel Manager synchronous test (stock/price update + cancellation flow)
Report accuracy (same day, consistency across different reports)
Authorization (department-based access, log records)

blocks.text: Pilot Plan (2–4 weeks): Rehearsal with “real data”

In Pilot, the goal is not only for the team to do the job “faster” but also to see how the system behaves in case of failure: what happens when the integration is interrupted, how are the reports affected, is there a manual fallback?
Reference Check: 7 clear questions to ask

items: How many months has it been used in a facility similar to our hotel type?
How is the support rate during the season?
What was the biggest surprise? (cost/integration/team adaptation)
How long did the data migration take?
Were there any problems with OTA/Channel Manager synchronization?
Are the reports sufficient to make decisions?
What would they do differently if they were re-elected?

blocks.text: ☑ Mini Check
“In the reference call, the question is not 'Are you satisfied?', but 'What was the biggest surprise?'"
What should I do?

items: Convert demo to “test” format rather than “presentation”
Set start-end criteria for the pilot (conditions for being considered successful)
Get reference questions in writing (later they become part of the contract)

sections.title: Transition from PMS Selection to Installation Project

blocks.text: Once the selection is complete, the real risk begins: the installation is seen as an “IT job” and if the operation does not take ownership, the project will drag on. The right approach: to manage the installation project as a business transformation.
Deployment Project Roadmap (example)

items: Kickoff: goals, scope, integration list, responsible
Data preparation: room types, rate plans, guest data set, contracts
Integration: OTA/Channel Manager, accounting, call center, RMS
Education: front desk, housekeeping, accounting, sales
Go-live: gradual transition, daily checklist
Stabilization: first 30 days report verification + process correction

blocks.text: “PMS Election File” Skeleton
A practical framework that can be given to your team with this article:

items: /01_Needs-Analysis/ (hotel type, must-have list, process flows)
/02_Integration-Map/ (PMS–OTA–Call Center–Accounting–RMS data flow chart)
/03_Demo-Pilot/ (KPI card, scenario list, test results)
/04_Agreement-SLA/ (scope, data migration, support, exit plan)
/05_Setup-Project/ (calendar, responsibility matrix, training plan)
/06_Reporting/ (standard reports, decision dashboard needs)

blocks.text: ☑ Mini Check
“If I don't have a selection file, my installation project grows uncontrolled.”
Technical Note:

items: Cloud-based PMS: You access the software over the internet; Updates are managed by the provider.
API integration: Two systems (e.g. PMS and call center) transfer certain data to each other “in a regular way”; This reduces manual copy-paste and reduces the risk of errors.

faq.q: How do I choose the right PMS for my hotel?
faq.a: First, extract the needs according to your hotel type (resort/city/boutique/villa). Then verify the modules and integrations (OTA/Channel Manager, call center, accounting, API). Measure the demo/pilot process with KPIs and clarify data migration and support/SLA in the contract.

faq.q: What should I pay attention to when choosing PMS?
faq.a: The three most critical areas are: requirements analysis, integration scope, and demo/pilot measurement. Ask for proof that so-called “existent” features work in real workflow. Support processes and data migration responsibilities during the season must be in writing.

faq.q: What is the difference between cloud PMS and classic PMS?
faq.a: Cloud PMS is accessed over the internet and updates are generally managed faster. Classic PMS can run on the hotel's own infrastructure; Although there is a control advantage in some structures, the maintenance/update burden may increase. Make the decision based on access need and integration/support model.

faq.q: How do I start the project process after PMS selection?
faq.a: Plan the installation as a “business transformation”: kickoff, data preparation, integration, training, go-live, and stabilization for the first 30 days. Joint ownership of operations and IT significantly reduces scope creep.

faq.q: Is Channel Manager mandatory or is PMS alone sufficient?
faq.a: In hotels with high OTA weight, Channel Manager plays a critical role in managing stock/price synchronization and overbooking risk. Although some PMSs offer internal channel modules, coverage and reliability must be tested. Make your selection via the “integration map”.

faq.q: What is the most common error in the data migration process?
faq.a: It is common for room types, rate plans, and guest data to mismatch “field reality.” Therefore, data cleaning and field mapping should be done before the pilot. Responsibility and schedule should be clear in the contract.

faq.q: What does "API" do in PMS integrations?
faq.a: API enables systems to transfer data to each other in a canonical manner; The risk of manual operation and error is reduced. For example, the call center can quickly access availability/price information, and accounting data becomes standard. If the integration scope is not written down, surprise costs may arise.

faq.q: What should I look for in the demo? Is the sales presentation sufficient?
faq.a: Turn demo into test case: intensive check-in, OTA cancellation flow, report accuracy and authorization. Make decisions with a “measured” approach, not a “demonstrated” approach. Sitting down with pilot results increases bargaining power.

faq.q: Which clauses in the contract are critical?
faq.a: Data migration scope, support/SLA, clear list of integrations and exit plan (data export) must be in writing. The emergency support procedure during the season should be defined separately. Most hidden costs arise when these items are not clear.

h1.title: How to Manage Hotel PMS Installation Project Step by Step?
h1.intro: PMS migration starts with “software installation work” in most hotels; but in real life it is an operation + revenue + distribution transformation. The most common result of migrations without the correct Project Plan is that the reception becomes clogged, inconsistencies occur in the channel/booking flow, and reports lose confidence. This guide makes the PMS Implementation process manageable from start to finish by dividing it into phases, roles and checklists.
quickSummary: 1) Clarify phase logic (Analysis → Data → Setup → Test → Go-Live → First 90 Days)
2) Put the role and responsibility in writing with RACI
3) Set a “single source file” standard for data preparation
4) Run test scenarios based on workday reality
5) Go-live without a cutover + rollback plan

sections.title: Stages of PMS Installation Project

blocks.text: PMS installation project varies depending on hotel size, number of integrations and seasonal schedule; but the phase logic is almost always the same. So “how long will it take?” before the question “which phases are there and who is responsible?” It is necessary to clarify the question. Especially in multi-room resorts such as Antalya / Belek / Side, "last minute" cutover attempts before the season create unnecessary risks.
6 Phase Logic (higher level)

items: Analysis & scope (goal, processes, integration map)
Data preparation (room plan, prices, company/agencies, user authorizations)
Setup & parameter (modules, business rules, user roles)
Testing (scenarios, integration tests, report validation)
Cutover & Go-Live (pilot date/hotel, transition plan, return plan)
First 90 days support (daily/weekly check, improvement cycle)

blocks.text: Here: “Project plan Gantt chart” (divided into weeks)

blocks.title: ☑ Mini Check
items: Go-Live is determined by the 'level of preparation', not the 'date'.

blocks.title: What should I do?
items: Write the phases, define the output of each phase (deliverable)
Evaluate cutover risk according to the season calendar (don't switch aggressively in high season)
Setting a go-live date without a “rollback”

sections.title: Project Team and Role Distribution

blocks.text: The most common mistake in PMS setup is “leaving it on one person”. In successful projects, the team is established across departments: the front office and operations take ownership of the workflow; It is on the table not for sales/revenue, but to maintain data accuracy and channel flow; clarifies accounting reporting and invoicing rules; IT/integration carries the process so that “systems talk”.
Minimum team (core)

items: Project owner (Sponsor): GM / hotel owner (priority and decision)
Project manager (PM): from internal team or consultant (schedule, risk, coordination)
Front Desk leader: check-in/out, folio, user flows
Sales/Booking: price plans, company/agents, contracts
Accounting: invoice, tax/rules, report validation
IT/Integration: PMS–Channel Manager–OTA–booking flow, API/connections
Revenue/Revenue (if applicable): price/reporting, demand/channel logic

blocks.text: RACI (Role & Responsibility Matrix)
Here: Role matrix visual (R=Responsible, A=Accountable, C=Consulted, I=Informed)

blocks.title: What should I do?
items: Take out a one-page RACI and have it signed at kickoff
Pin project communication: weekly meeting + daily brief status (in go-live week)
“Who is the decision maker?” clarify your question (especially cutover decisions)

sections.title: Data Collection and Preliminary Preparation

blocks.text: The success of a PMS installation is often determined by data quality, not “setup screens.” If the room plan, price plans, company/agencies, user authorizations and business rules are messy, the post-go-live team will have difficulty, no matter how good the PMS is. This phase is the “most often tedious but most critical” part of the project.
Main data sets to be collected

items: Room inventory: room types, room features, room–floor plan
Price & packages: BAR, package, season, min. accommodation, cancellation policies
Company/agencies: current cards, contract conditions, commission structures
Users & authorizations: department-based roles, log and approval flows
Tax/invoice rules: one-to-one verification with accounting
Booking flow: sources, channel breakdown, manual/automatic processes

blocks.text: Here: “Data Preparation Control Table” (responsible, due date, status)
Pilot Hotel / Pilot Date selection

In multi-facility buildings or high season pressures, choosing “Pilot Hotel” or “pilot date” reduces the risk. The pilot shows how the system behaves on real data; It also provides training and familiarization time for the team before the cutover.

blocks.title: ☑ Mini Check
items: If there is no pilot, go-live becomes a pilot.

blocks.title: What should I do?
items: Uniform data templates (single source file logic)
Get the "price/contract" agreement in writing between sales-accounting-front office
Coinciding the pilot date with the season calendar (especially before the high season in Antalya/Belek/Side)

sections.title: Module and Parameter Setup

blocks.text: This phase is not “we opened the screens”; It means translating the hotel's business rules into PMS. Proceeding on a module-by-module basis both simplifies the testing process and makes the impact of changes controllable. At this point, it is essential to consider PMS together with Channel Manager, OTA, reservation management and, if necessary, Call Center flows.
Module based progression example

items: Front Desk: check-in/out flow, folio, deposit, guest card
Reservation management: price/availability, options, cancellation/no-show rules
Housekeeping: room statuses, task distribution, checklists
Accounting: invoice templates, revenue accounts, report format
Integration: PMS–Channel Manager–OTA sync, API connections

blocks.text: Here: “PMS module map diagram”
Technical note (plain language)

items: API integration: Systems transfer certain data to each other in a canonical manner; reduces manual error.
Parameter setup: Defining the hotel's business rules (cancellation requirement, tax, commission, authority) in the PMS.

blocks.title: ☑ Mini Check
items: If you do not write the rules correctly in the PMS, the team will live with the 'wrong right'.

blocks.title: What should I do?
items: Validate rules by department: are sales–accounting–front office on the same page?
Document the scope of integration (which data, in which direction, with what frequency)
Track installation changes with the 'change log'

sections.title: Testing, Go-Live and First 90 Days of Support

blocks.text: The test is “does it work?” not; “does it work in the reality of the workday?” is the question. In order for the reception to survive on the Go-Live day, test scenarios (normal day, busy day, cancellation/no-show, channel synchronization) should be worked out in advance. Then comes the Cutover plan: if the steps to go-live and the rollback plan are not clear, the risk grows.
Things that must be tested before Go-Live

items: Check-in/out end-to-end (reservation → folio → invoice)
Channel Manager/OTA stock/price sync + cancellation flow
User permissions (who sees/changes what)
Report verification (“same fact” as accounting)
Integration error scenario (what happens if the connection drops?)

blocks.text: Here: “Go-live checklist table” (before/after)
First 90 days support: real success starts here

First 90 days; It is the period when small daily problems accumulate, processes are established and "trust in PMS" is established. In projects that are not well managed, confusion grows on the reception and revenue side during this period; In well-managed projects, risks are minimized through systematic control.

blocks.title: What should I do?
items: Establish a daily 15-minute “quick state” rhythm during the go-live week
Do a report verification cycle for the first 30 days (accounting + PM + front office)
Create weekly checklist + improvement backlog for 90 days

sections.title: Where should I start with PMS installation?

blocks.text: The best start to PMS installation is the project plan and role distribution, not software screens. First establish the project team and clarify the phases and deliverables; Then collect room/price/company data from a single source. Identify demo/test scenarios and write the Cutover–Go-Live plan with the “fallback step”. Finally, define the daily/weekly check rhythm for the first 90 days.

blocks.text: The most common risk in multi-room resorts such as Antalya / Belek / Side is the “last week” go-live decision before high season. This approach reduces training/tracking time and reduces tolerance for error in the reception–channel–accounting chain. Safer scenario: transition to a low-risk period with a pilot date, then entering the season with a “stabilized” system.

Pilot + make a gradual transition plan (Pilot Hotel or pilot date)
Book an “intensive support” plan for the first 14 days after go-live

faq.q: How to manage PMS installation project?
faq.a: Break it down into phases: analysis, data preparation, setup, testing, cutover/go-live, and first 90 days of support. Clarify role distribution with RACI, put test scenarios in writing and establish a daily/weekly control rhythm after go-live.

faq.q: How long does PMS installation take?
faq.a: Duration varies depending on hotel size, number of integrations and data readiness. However, the phase logic is generally the same: if data preparation and testing are done well, go-live risk decreases; Rushed projects take longer after go-live.

faq.q: Which departments should be in the project team?
faq.a: At a minimum, there should be GM/PM, front office, sales/reservations, accounting and IT/integration. Because rules, data accuracy, and integrations are cross-departmental. Ownership of the operation remains weak in projects managed with “IT only”.

faq.q: What should be tested before go-live?
faq.a: Check-in/out end-to-end, Channel Manager/OTA synchronization (stock/price/cancellation), user authorizations, report validation and integration error scenarios should be tested. Additionally, cutover steps and a return plan should be written.

faq.q: What is cutover and why is it important?
faq.a: Cutover is a transition plan in which the transition from the old system to the new PMS is managed step by step. Without the right plan, chaos will increase on go-live day. A good cutover plan controls risk with a rollback step.

h1.title: How to Manage Data Migration and Cleansing Process in PMS Migration?
h1.intro: PMS migration is often evaluated through screens and modules; But the invisible side of success is data. Moving "garbage", "duplicate" or missing records exactly as they are in the old system is not a faster operation in the new PMS; produces a mess faster. This guide divides the data migration process into data selection → cleaning → data mapping → test import → verification → post-go-live quality control steps, ensuring that both IT and operations proceed with the same plan.

ctaPrimary.label: Request PMS Data Migration Process Analysis
ctaPrimary.text: Classifies data fields together (moved/cleared/not moved), outputs mapping + test plan; You reduce the risk of post-go-live report noise.

sections.title: Why is Data Migration Critical?

blocks.text: Data migration appears to be a “one-time” step in a PMS Implementation project; but its effect lasts for years. If guest cards are faulty, personalization is broken, if the company/agents are dirty, trade reports are confused, and if price agreements are conveyed incorrectly, the revenue side takes risks. Moreover, if data quality decreases, the team “blames” the new PMS and adoption suffers.
Example (directional expression): CRM/guest cards migrated without data cleansing; creates significant noise in reporting and personalization projects; Correct migration has the opposite effect.
☑ Mini Check: “Will we trust the reports after 1 month in the new PMS?”

blocks.title: What should I do?
items: Define migration as “data quality project” rather than “IT export/import”
“Which data do we carry and why?” answer the question in writing
Plan to use initial post-go-live reports as a quality control tool

sections.title: Which Data Should Be Moved, Which Should Be Cleared?

blocks.text: The biggest mistake is the "since we're moving, let's carry everything" approach. The right approach is to divide the data into operational necessity, commercial continuity and analytical value layers and make a "movable/cleaned/not moved" decision at each layer. Additionally, the issue of “history” is critical: each hotel must decide how much of the history to keep alive in the new PMS.
Data classification: Movable / Clear / Immovable

items: Portable (core): room plan, rate plans, company/agents, user authorizations, active booking/stay rules
Moved clean: guest cards, CRM fields, history selection (with rule set)
Real estate (usually): unnecessary personal notes, old codes that are no longer used, “undefined” free text fields (KVKK can be risky)

blocks.text: Here: “Data field list table” (field name, source, target, moved/cleared/immovable, responsible)
Antalya/Belek scenario: guest card data accumulated over the years

The most common situation in hotels that have been using the same PMS for years, such as Antalya / Belek, is this: the number of guest cards is large, but a significant part of the records are duplicate, missing or inconsistent. So instead of “carrying it all”; It is safer to move with deduplication, field standard and history limit.
☑ Mini Check: “Will the data I move improve the decision quality in the new PMS, or will it produce noise?”

blocks.title: What should I do?
items: Separate data fields into 3 classes: moved/cleared/not moved
Set history limit (Assumption: active + selected valued history)
Write dedup rule on guest cards (phone/email/name-surname combinations)

sections.title: Data Transfer Steps from Old System to PMS

blocks.text: The success of migration depends on the clarity of the steps. Instead of the "we import it from Excel" approach, it is necessary to proceed with the trio of data mapping + controlled import + validation.
Step-by-step migration flow (hands-on)

items: Legacy System inventory: what resources are available? (old PMS, Excel, different department files)
Field mapping (Data Mapping): source field → target PMS field → conversion rule
Cleaning rules: duplicate, free space, format standard (phone, country, email, etc.)
Trial import in test environment: small sample + controlled expansion
Verification reports: count check, sample record check, critical report consistency
Production import + log: errors and corrections are recorded
Post-go-live quality control: initial reports + correction cycle with field feedback

blocks.text: Here: “Old→New field mapping diagram”
☑ Mini Check: “If I don't have a mapping document, data migration is a matter of luck.”

blocks.title: What should I do?
items: Keep the mapping document in a single file (think of it like version control)
Start test imports with “sample”; then enlarge coverage
Keep an error log for each import (who fixed it, what was fixed)

sections.title: Testing, Verification and Error Lists

blocks.text: Migration tests are just “installed?” is not; “Are the reports and workflow correct after installation?” It is a jug. Therefore, verification should include both IT and operations checklist.
Migration test checklist (example)

items: Number of source records vs number of target records (tolerant control)
Are the required fields filled? (room type, price plan, company/agencies)
Format accuracy (phone, email, country code)
Duplicate check result (how many records were merged/discarded)
Sample guest card: is the “single registration” behavior correct?
Example booking/price: is the rule working correctly?
Report validation: basic consistency with accounting

blocks.text: Here: “Migration test checklist” image + “Error & fix log” example
Error & correction log logic (minimum)

items: Error ID / Date
Source data / target field
Error type (format/empty/duplicate/mapping)
Corrective action
Person in charge
Retest result

blocks.text: ☑ Mini Check: “If I don't have an error log, I will reproduce the same error.”

blocks.title: What should I do?
items: Don't say 'ok' without an error log
Select a sample of 20–30 critical records for verification (department based)
Use initial reports as a “quality alert”

sections.title: Maintaining Data Quality After Migration

blocks.text: If data quality is not maintained after go-live, old problems will return within a few months. Data Quality requires a minimum “rhythm”: standard entry rules, periodic cleaning and reporting control.
Simple but effective quality rhythm

items: Weekly: duplicate check (guest card, company/agencies)
Monthly: space occupancy and format report
90 days: mapping and business rule review (what's breaking?)
Training: mini training for the 3 most common mistake areas

blocks.text: ☑ Mini Check: “If there is no quality rule, you need data discipline, not PMS.”

blocks.title: What should I do?
items: Publish “Required fields” standard (operation + sales)
Define a simple Data Quality dashboard (occupancy, duplicate, error trend)
Update “data dictionary” every 90 days

sections.title: How should you manage the PMS data migration process?

items: Classify data: decide whether to move/clear/immovable
Perform Data Mapping: document source→target domain + conversion rule
Set cleaning rules: duplicate, format, free space, history limit
Apply test import: sample → proceed with expansion approach
Verify: count + sample record + critical report consistency
Establish quality rhythm after go-live: error log + report control loop

blocks.label: Download Data Field Map & Migration Test Checklist
blocks.text: What does it do? Allows IT and operations to manage field mapping, cleaning rule, and test verification steps together in the same file; reduce post-go-live data noise [sic].

sections.title: How will I move data from the old PMS?

items: Decide which data you will move first (they will be moved by core + flush).
Map the fields: source field → new PMS field → transformation rule.
Clean duplicate and garbage records; Start with a small import in the test environment.
Keep an error log, fix it and re-import it.
Verify data and establish quality rhythm with initial post-go-live reports.

sections.title: KVKK Short Technical Note

blocks.text: "Habitually" moving unnecessary personal data during data migration is both risky and costly. With the retention period approach in accordance with KVKK; Move only the data required for operational/commercial continuity, leaving out unnecessary fields. Reduce risk with masking (e.g. partial view instead of full data) and access authorization (role-based access) where necessary.

steps: Mark each data field as moved/cleared/unmoved on the field map.

faq.q: What data should be migrated during PMS migration?
faq.a: Core data required for operation and commercial continuity (room plan, price plans, company/agencies, user authorizations) must be moved; Areas such as guest cards should be cleaned and moved. Unnecessary/high risk free text personal notes should not be carried in most cases.

faq.q: How do I clear faulty records in the old system?
faq.a: First set the duplicate rule (phone/email/name combinations), then apply the format standard (phone/email/country). Identify empty critical areas and make a “complete/discard” decision. Document the cleaning rules and verify them in the test import.

faq.q: How are data migration tests performed?
faq.a: Start with a small sample in the test environment, increase the scope after the mapping is verified. Perform count check, sample record check, and critical report consistency verification. Keep an error log for each import and apply a fix-retest cycle.

faq.q: How do I check data quality after go-live?
faq.a: Establish a report verification rhythm for the first 30 days (accounting + operations). Get weekly duplicate check and domain occupancy report. Publish mini-training and standard entry rules for the 2–3 areas where most mistakes are made.

faq.q: What should be taken into consideration in data migration in terms of KVKK?
faq.a: Do not carry unnecessary personal data, narrow the scope with a retention period approach. Implement role-based access and masking if necessary. For KVKK requirements, connect to the data security page.

h1.title: PMS Training Plan: How to Prepare Reception and Operations Teams for the New System?
h1.intro: Saying that we "installed" the new PMS does not mean that the team "started using it correctly". Errors such as wrong date, wrong price, wrong room/contract on the reception and operation side; It may cause revenue and reputation risks in a short time. Therefore, the training plan is not a “usage demonstration”; It is a project part that includes role-based onboarding, scenario practices, measurement-evaluation and first 60-day support rhythm. This guide provides a Training Playbook for the HR + operations + project team to implement together.
quickSummary: 1. Determine the target audience: Handle Reception, Reservation Team, Revenue, Accounting roles separately

4. Measure: quiz/certificate + set delegation criteria
5. Establish a follow-up cycle: Q&A + refresh sessions + question pool for the first 30–60 days

sections.title: Why is PMS Training the Heart of the Project?

blocks.text: In PMS Implementation, technical installation alone does not bring success; What determines success is the integration of the system into the daily workflow as "correct behavior". This can only happen with training: the right person learns the right module, rehearses the right scenario, and goes live with the right authority. If training gets "stuck in between" due to shift pressure in busy operation destinations such as Antalya/Belek/Side, errors increase; If the training is linked to the project plan, risks are reduced.

blocks.title: Risk warning
items: Unauthorized or untrained PMS use; Incorrect price, date and room transactions may result in serious revenue and reputation risks.

blocks.text: Classroom education explains the concept; hands-on training builds muscle memory; On-the-job carries it to real life. The most effective model is a balanced combination of these. Training would be incomplete without "role-play", especially in operational roles such as reception.

blocks.text: 2 weeks after the training, the team asked “where is his grade?” If it returns to the question, the documentation is weak. That's why training materials should not be a one-time presentation, but a living guide: short workflow cards, list of common errors, mini video recordings, step-by-step guide with screenshots.

faq.q: How should PMS training be planned?
faq.a: Plan by role: create separate content sets for reception, reservations, accounting and revenue teams. Organize short sessions + applied scenarios according to shift patterns. Authorize with a mini quiz and set up a refresh/Q&A cycle for the first 60 days.

faq.q: Which department should learn which PMS modules?

faq.q: How long and in what format should I do the training?
faq.a: Instead of one long training, use the on-the-job model of short classroom lecture + practice in a test environment + role-play + go-live week. Scheduling repeated sessions by shift reduces forgetting.

faq.q: How is the post-training support process managed?
faq.a: Brief daily Q&A is recommended for the first 10 days, then weekly refresh sessions are recommended. Collect a pool of questions and conduct mini training on the 3 most common mistakes. Review authorization and documentation on days 30 and 60.

sections.title: Common Mistakes: Where Do We Go Wrong?

blocks.text: In PMS Implementation projects, errors are generally concentrated in 4 areas: process/data, training/change, integration/testing, timing/seasonality. Even problems that seem like “a single technical error” often arise from a combination of these four areas.

blocks.title: Mini Check
blocks.text: “Is it PMS or lack of plan + data + training + testing?”
What should I do?

items: Categorize bugs: data, process, training, integration, season
Add “risk–impact” note for each error
Link preventive actions to the project plan

sections.title: What are the common mistakes in PMS installation?

blocks.text: The list below is “What should I not do during PMS transition?” The clear answer to the question is:

items: Mistaking Data Migration for export/import (no cleaning and mapping)
Moving any data without setting the history limit
Move guest cards without clearing duplicate/junk data
Not verifying rules (price, cancellation, tax, commission) between departments
Compressing training into go-live (especially Reception)
Just train the reception and skip the accounting/booking
Going live without an authorization plan (unauthorized operations)
Not establishing an Integration Test plan (no scenario-based testing)
Not testing Channel Manager/OTA integration adequately
Not managing the double enrollment period with the old system (who gets in where?)
Making an impromptu go-live in the middle of the season or very close to the season
Not establishing a support rhythm for the first 30–90 days after go-live (no tickets/backlogs)

sections.title: Errors on the Process and Data Side

blocks.text: Mistake 1: Underestimating Data Migration
False: “We take the data, throw it away, then fix it.”
Correct: Movable/cleared/immovable decision + mapping + test import + verification report.
Practical solution: Migration test checklist + error/fix log.
Mistake 2: Moving everything (no history limit)
Wrong: Moving years of data without filtering → report noise.
Correct: Selecting active and valuable history; Leaving out unnecessary areas.
Practical solution: “History policy” (Assumption: active + selected valuable period).
Mistake 3: Not validating rules across departments
Wrong: Sales knows separate rules, accounting has separate rules → conflict in live.
Correct: Single “dictionary of rules” (cancellation/tax/commission/rate plan) + signed confirmation.
What should I do?

items: Collect rule dictionary in one file
Get report verification criteria in writing before live with accounting

sections.title: Training and Change Management Mistakes

blocks.text: Mistake 4: Leaving training until the last day
Wrong: “We teach when the system comes.”
Correct: Role-based PMS Training + scenario/role-play + mini-quiz + delegation.
Practical solution: 2-week training sprint before Go-live + 60-day refresh rhythm.
Mistake 5: Training only the reception
Incorrect: Accounting/booking untrained → report/price/rules are broken.
Correct: Reception + Reservation Team + Accounting + Revenue are included in the same plan.
What should I do?

items: Create a role matrix
Identify a list of 10–15 critical actions for each role
Tie your competency plan to training success

sections.title: Integration and Testing Errors

blocks.text: Mistake 6: Going live without an Integration Test plan
Wrong: “We checked it one by one and it works.”
Correct: Scenario-based testing: heavy check-in, cancel/no-show, OTA sync, report validation, error scenario.
Practical solution: Test scenarios + acceptance criteria (pass/fail).
Mistake 7: Not testing the OTA/Channel Manager enough
Incorrect: Stock/price discrepancy → overbooking/revenue risk.
Correct: Separate test suite + monitoring for channel management and OTA integration.
Technical note (locked): Channel management and OTA integration tests are among the most critical control points that determine go-live risk.

items: Internal links: /tr/pms-ota/ota-integration
Internal links: /tr/pms-ota/channel-management

sections.title: Road Map to Avoid These Mistakes

items: Perform Risk–Impact analysis: seasonal calendar + data volume + number of integrations
Lock data & migration plan: mapping + cleanup + test import + validation
Set up the test plan scenario-wise: integration + channel/OTA + report verification
Manage training based on role: mini-exam + authorization + go-live shadowing
Establish a support rhythm for 30–90 days after go-live: ticket/backlog + weekly check + refresh

sections.title: What should I not do during PMS transition?

items: Do not move the data without cleaning and mapping.
Do not pass the integration and OTA/Channel Manager tests by eye.
Don't squeeze education into go-live; Delegate with role-based plan and mini quiz.
Don't do an impromptu go-live during or too close to the season; Have the cutover plan written.
Do not consider the project "done" without a support rhythm of 30–90 days after go-live.

sections.title: “Go-live at the wrong time” in Antalya/Belek/Side/Bodrum

blocks.text: In destinations with high seasonality such as Antalya / Belek / Side / Bodrum, "system change in high season" is the most expensive form of risk. Even small errors become magnified due to reception density, channel volume and team turnover. Safer approach: off-season/pilot period + controlled cutover + vigorous testing and training.

faq.q: What are the most common mistakes in PMS installation?
faq.a: The most common mistakes; Moving the data without cleaning it, leaving the training to the last day, missing integration and OTA/Channel Manager tests, and trying an unprepared go-live in the middle of the season. These errors often lead to report inconsistency, reception confusion, and revenue risk.

faq.q: Is it right to switch to PMS in high season?
faq.a: Go-live is risky in high season in destinations with high seasonality; Small disruptions quickly escalate. Safer approach; off-season/pilot period, controlled cutover and strong test-training plan.

faq.q: What mistakes should I avoid during data migration?
faq.a: Importing without mapping, moving duplicate/garbage data, and not setting a history limit are the most common mistakes. It is necessary to proceed with trial imports and verification reports in the test environment.

faq.q: How should I plan integration tests?
faq.a: Plan scenario-wise: busy check-in, cancellation/no-show, OTA sync, report verification and error scenario. The test should not be considered "complete" without acceptance criteria (pass/fail) and error log.

sections.title: What is OTA integration and why is it important?

blocks.text: OTA integration is the automatic synchronous transfer of hotel inventory and price information (and often restrictions) to channels such as Booking.com, Expedia, Agoda. “Integration” here is not a one-time connection; It is a constant stream of data running at the heart of daily operation. If this flow is not designed correctly, what appears to be a “sale open” situation may actually mean incorrect prices, incorrect availability, or restrictions not working.
Why is it important? (Summary effects)

items: Reduces the risk of overbooking: prevents the same room type from overlapping across multiple channels.
Provides price consistency: web / OTA / B2B channel prices are kept under control.
It reduces the operational burden: the reception and reservation team performs “control and optimization” instead of “manual updating”.
It accelerates revenue management: you react faster to seasonal, occupancy and demand changes.

blocks.text: Short answer block of 4–5 items (as required by AEO)

items: OTA integration carries rate + inventory information to OTAs via PMS/channel manager.
The “single source of truth” is determined: PMS or channel manager is the main management panel.
OTAs send back the incoming reservation/cancellation; PMS and reporting are fed correctly.
Correct setup means price consistency + availability accuracy + working constraints.
Improper installation often produces the “invisible error”: lost revenue and guest dissatisfaction.

blocks.text: Mini example (resort scenario)

Think of a resort in Belek: You opened a last minute campaign for room type A. If the campaign price is updated in Booking and remains in Expedia, two different price perceptions occur on the same day. This situation is not just a loss of income; The guest asks “why is it different?” It also turns into a reputation risk.
☑ Mini Check: “Is there integration or not?”

items: When I update the price/room on at least one channel, is the other channel updated within 5–15 minutes?
Do the restrictions (min stay / stop sell) behave the same on every channel?
Is reservation cancellation processed correctly in PMS?

blocks.title: What should I do?
items: Determine the “single source of truth” (PMS or channel manager?).
Create a “matching map” for room types and rate plans.
Define 3 basic test scenarios: price change, stop-sell, cancellation.
With internal links (depth of knowledge): Review the PMS setup and channel management pages.

sections.title: Basic logic of Booking & Expedia integration

blocks.text:
Although Booking and Expedia have different extranet habits, their integration logic sits on the same core:
	• Room type
	• Rate plan (price plan)
	• Availability/inventory
	• Restrictions (restrictions: min stay, CTA, CTD, stop sell)

Each of these parts lives as a “definition” in the PMS or channel manager. It also has an equivalent on the OTA side. Integration means “the correct match between definitions”.

The most common mistake: Mismatching rate plans and restrictions by saying "the room type is the same". Result: you think the system is working, but promotions, minimum stays or closing rules behave differently across a channel.
Let's simplify the basic logic (in hotel decision language):

items: Is “what I will sell” a room type or a package? (room vs package)
With which rate plans does my “price logic” proceed? (refund / non-refund / early booking)
What is my “rule set”? (min stay, stop sell, arrival/departure closure)
Do these definitions find a one-to-one correspondence in OTA?

blocks.text: Mini example (Antalya summer season):

You set a 3-night minimum stay rule in Antalya during peak summer months. If it works on Booking but not on Expedia, you will get a 1 night sale from Expedia. This disrupts the operation (room schedule, housekeeping rhythm) and skews revenue management.
☑ Mini Check: “Rate plan match”

items: Are Refundable/Non-refundable rate plans individually matched?
Is there a separate plan for promotions (EB, last minute) or a discount on the same plan?
Are the min stay / stop sell rules consistent on a channel-by-channel basis?

blocks.title: What should I do?
items: Organize rate plans with a “list → simplify → match” approach.
Decide to manage promotions in one place (the CM side is generally safer).
Establish a weekly check-in rhythm for “consistency across channels.”

sections.title: How does the PMS + Channel Manager + OTA trio work?

blocks.text:
This trio is actually a “distribution chain”. The most practical explanation is this:
	• PMS: The “operational reality” of the hotel (check-in/out, room status, guest records, stay flow).
	• Channel Manager: “distribution and rule engine” (rate, inventory, restriction distribution) to sales channels.
	• OTA: The showcase from which the request comes (visibility, conversion, reservation, cancellation).

Let's clarify the relationship per AIO: PMS → sends → Rates & Inventory → to Channel Manager → distributes → to OTA.
In some installations, prices/restrictions are managed from the channel manager, not from the PMS; PMS only makes the reservation “last registration”. The important thing is to establish a single management point and avoid conflict.
Critical decision: Where is the “single source of truth”?

items: If the PMS is weak in price/constraint management, the channel manager becomes the center.
If PMS offers strong RMS/price rules, PMS can be the center; CM distributes.
Writing rules on both sides: the most common mistake.

blocks.text: Mini example (Side / Belt operation):

At the hotel in Side, the reception closes rooms via PMS and launches a campaign via marketing CM. If two different rules are written on two different panels on the same day, the sale will be closed in one OTA and remain open in the other. This produces a crisis such as “sales came from a channel, but the room was actually closed.”
☑ Mini Check: “Single source fact test”

items: Do we make price/restriction changes from a single panel?
Is the same rule written in two places? (PMS+CM)
Did we measure update latency? (5–15 minutes normal, hours risk)

blocks.title: What should I do?
items: In the organization, “who manages which panel?” Put the question in writing.
Clarify PMS/CM roles: PMS operation, CM deployment, and rules engine.
Plan at least 1 “end-to-end test day” (price change + stop sell + cancellation).

sections.title: Basic rules of price and inventory flow

blocks.text:
The “quality standard” of integration lies in these two questions:
	1. Where is the price managed? (rate rules, promotions, packages)
	2. Where is the inventory updated? (availability, allotment, stop-sell)
1) Rate rules – minimum set

items: Rate plan hierarchy: BAR, non-refundable, early booking, last minute…
Parity management: Control logic of Web/OTA/B2B prices
Commission impact: A look at net income after OTA commission (net ADR)

blocks.text: 2) Inventory rules – minimum set

items: Availability based on room type: “Sellable rooms” logic, not “total rooms”
Stop-sell discipline: immediate closure in case of maintenance/operation/overbooking risk
Allotment and free selling: Clarifying not moving B2B allotments to OTAs

blocks.text: 3) Restriction – the part that most hotels skip (the mini part that closes the Competitor gap)

A lot of content remains at “room and price”; The part that makes the difference is the correct management of constraints:
	• Min stay (minimum stay)
	• CTA/CTD (arrival/departure closure)
	• Stop sell (full closure)
	• Close to arrival / close to departure
When these rules do not work correctly, you may think you are “managing occupancy” but you will receive unbalanced sales by channel.

Key Statistics / Data Point (smoothed benchmark):
In some resort hotels, it is seen that a significant portion of online sales (where the OTA weight is high in most benchmarks) can be managed synchronously and without errors, with properly designed OTA integration; The critical thing is not to chase the rate, but to bring the cost of error close to zero.
☑ Mini Check: “3 layer rule”

items: Are rate plans simple or multiplied? (unnecessary plans risk)
Are inventory updates in one place?
Is our restriction set written and consistent across channels?

blocks.title: What should I do?
items: Simplify rate plans and pull them into the “main plan + promotion” structure.
Manage the inventory with the logic of “sellable room” and write a stop-sell procedure.
Pour the restrictions into a table and test them on each channel (min stay + CTA/CTD).

blocks.text: Identify price-inventory-constraint errors in your PMS/CM installation before they escalate and get a correction plan.

sections.title: Impact of OTA integration on hotel revenue

blocks.text: While OTA integration may seem like a “technical setup,” it has three clear outcomes on the revenue side:
1) Visibility → Conversion effect

Visibility in OTAs is not only through advertising; It's also about accurate availability and competitive, consistent pricing. A room type that appears “closed” due to a mismatch lowers your visibility. This means fewer clicks, fewer reservations.
2) Operation error cost (hidden cost)

items: When overbooking, you don't just lose "a guest"; You generate an exponentially growing cost, including transfers, upgrades, refunds, reputation and staff time.
In price discrepancy, trust is broken when the guest sees "two different prices for the same room".

blocks.text: 3) Revenue management speed

The right integration shifts your team's time from “manual updates” to “revenue optimization.” Fast action makes a difference, especially in destinations such as Bodrum, Kemer and Alanya, where seasonal demand changes sharply.
Mini example (Bodrum weekend request):
Weekend demand increased; You increased the BAR. If the update is delayed in a channel, you will be undersold and lose revenue on a “high demand day”.
☑ Mini Check: “Revenue impact measurement set”

items: Is there clear channel-based ADR tracking? (after commission)
Are overbooking / relocations recorded?
Are price discrepancy complaints monitored?

blocks.title: What should I do?
items: Establish channel reporting with a “net revenue” perspective (post commission).
Make error cost visible: keep an overbooking/relocation log.
Catch invisible errors with a weekly “rules and constraints” audit.

items: Has PMS release/integration competency been verified?
Was the channel manager selection made according to the number of OTAs and needs?
Have room types and rate plans been simplified?
Is restriction set (min stay/CTA/CTD/stop sell) written?
Have test scenarios been determined (price, stop-sell, cancellation, no-show)?
Are the roles clear: who is PMS, who leads the CM panel?

As a result, the team moves the integration to the “we measured and verified” level rather than the “we connected it” level.

faq.q: What is OTA integration?
faq.a: OTA integration is the automatic transfer of price and availability information to channels such as Booking and Expedia via PMS and/or channel manager. The goal is to ensure consistency across channels and reduce manual errors.

faq.q: How does Booking and Expedia integration work for hotels?
faq.a: Room types and rate plans are matched in systems; Then rate + inventory + restrictions are distributed to OTA. Reservation and cancellation feedback falls into the PMS and feeds the operation.

faq.q: How does the PMS + channel manager + OTA trio work?
faq.a: PMS keeps operational records, channel manager works as a distribution and rule engine, and OTA is the sales showcase and demand source. The most critical issue is to decide where the "single source of truth" is.

faq.q: How does OTA integration impact hotel revenue and visibility?
faq.a: Proper integration drives visibility and conversion; Revenue loss is reduced through price consistency and accurate availability. Error costs (overbooking, wrong price) are reduced.

faq.q: Is a channel manager required for OTA integration?
faq.a: Yes in many hotels; because it provides channel-based rule management and ease of distribution. However, if your PMS's integration capability is strong, simpler installations are also possible in some scenarios.

faq.q: Will it end overbooking completely?
faq.a: It would not be correct to say "zeroes" alone; But with correct synchronous and single panel management, the risk drops dramatically. The real assurance is the test cases and monitoring rhythm.

faq.q: What is the most common installation mistake?
faq.a: Price/restrictions are written in both PMS and channel manager and matches remain incomplete. These conflicts produce inconsistent behavior across channels.

faq.q: What to watch in the first week after integration?
faq.a: Update delay, price consistency, stop-sell behavior and cancellation flow should be monitored. In addition, channel-based net ADR and overbooking events should be recorded.

sections.title: Clarify the installation flow: mapping + testing = quality

blocks.text: The goal of this guide is not to “count panel steps”; It is about establishing a discipline that will catch errors before they go live. Quality of integration; Mapping accuracy is measured by the test booking matrix and the first 30 days monitoring cadence.

blocks.label: Get OTA Integration Setup Consultancy

sections.title: Preparation of Booking and Expedia accounts

blocks.text: Before setup begins, your “definitions” should be clear in Booking and the Expedia extranet. Because mapping is linked to definition; If the definition is blurry, the mapping will also be blurry. If you make this preparation correctly, decisions will be accelerated on the PMS and channel manager side.

items: Room types: In the resort scenario, the main room + variations (view, capacity, concept) should be defined simply.
Price plans: Plans such as refundable / non-refundable / early booking / last minute should be placed on the naming standard.
Policies: cancellation, prepayment, minimum stay and package contents should be clarified.
Market/market combinations: e.g. If certain markets in Antalya/Belek have different minimum night rules, it should be written down.

blocks.title: Mini Check: Extranet readiness check
items: Are room type names clear enough to be compatible with PMS/CM?
Are rate plans (refund/non-refund/promotion) separate?
Are cancellation and prepayment policies written?
Has the min stay / stop-sell approach been determined?

blocks.title: What should I do?
items: Simplify room types with a “sellable product” mentality.
Standardize rate plan names (same logic on every channel).
Put the policies and constraints in a table and approve them with the team.

sections.title: PMS and channel manager settings: connection and roles

blocks.text: There are two critical decisions at this stage: (1) What is the only source of truth? (Is the price/restriction in PMS or channel manager?) (2) Who manages which panel? (RM, reservations, front desk, agency/IT). General practice: PMS becomes the reality of the operation, the channel manager is the distribution and rules engine. The important thing is not to write rules in two places.
Minimum set for connection installation

items: PMS ↔ Channel manager API/connector activation
Channel list: Booking + Expedia (and others if available)
Update frequency/latency expectation (in minute level)

blocks.title: Mini Check: Single panel discipline
items: Will price/restriction changes be made from a single panel?
From which system will room availability (inventory) be updated?
Will update latency be measured? (go-live KPI)

blocks.title: What should I do?
items: “Who controls what and from where?” Write the document on one page.
PMS operation; Position CM as the distribution/rules engine.
Set a latency measurement target before go-live.

sections.title: Room & rate mapping: step by step installation list

blocks.text: How to integrate Booking and Expedia? (5–7 steps)

items: Match room types (RoomType): Link room types in PMS/CM to their room counterparts in OTA.
Match rate plans: Link BAR, refundable, non-refundable, promotional plans to OTA rates.
Define the restriction set: Determine how restrictions such as min stay, stop-sell, CTA/CTD will be distributed to which channels.
Verify tax/commission logic: net/gross price approach and check included/excluded items.
Lock inventory update rule: make clear salable room logic, allotment and closing procedure.
Perform a “mapping audit” with sample combinations: create a quick verification table for each room type × each rate plan.
Move to the test booking stage: run all critical scenarios before going live.

blocks.text: Relational expression: RoomType → mappedTo → OTA Room, RatePlan → mappedTo → OTA Rate; TestReservation → validates → mapping + restriction + cancellation flow.

blocks.caption: Table: Room & Rate Mapping + Test Reservation Matrix (template)

blocks.title: Mini Check: Mapping verification
items: Is each room type matched to the correct product on the OTA?
Is each rate plan linked to the correct price logic?
Are restrictions consistent on a channel-by-channel basis?

blocks.title: What should I do?
items: Conduct mapping with two separate checklists: "room" and "price".
Simplify plans rather than multiply promotions.
Run a channel-based mini-test for “constraints” at least once.

sections.title: Test reservations and error scenarios (critical section)

blocks.text: Most competing content stays superficial here: it says “test” but doesn't tell you what to test. The part that makes the difference should be your test booking matrix and error scenario procedure.
Minimum test set (per hotel)

items: Test-1: Room type A + Rate plan BAR → reservation → PMS drop control
Test-2: Room type A + Non-refundable → payment/prepayment logic check
Test-3: Min stay rule → Does it behave correctly in OTA?
Test-4: Stop-sell → does the sale close, how long does it take to close?
Test-5: Cancellation → Is OTA cancellation reflected correctly in PMS?
Test-6: Is the date change / modification → rate difference and availability update correct?

blocks.text: Error scenarios (resort facts)

items: Wrong price appears → RatePlan mapping/taxes/rounding
The sale does not close → stop-sell distribution is delayed or written from the wrong panel
Overbooking risk → inventory update/two panel conflict
Cancellation flow broken → connector setting/reservation status mapping

blocks.title: Mini Check: Test discipline
items: Has every room × every rate plan combination been tested?
Has the cancellation and modification flow been checked?
Have stop-sell and min stay restrictions been verified?

blocks.title: What should I do?
items: Plan tests with a “combination matrix” (room × rate).
When you catch an error, first check the mapping and then the taxes/restrictions.
Before going live, the "test report" should be written.

sections.title: Go live and monitoring for the first 30 days (stabilization plan)

blocks.text: Going live is not a technical “switch”; is the stabilization process. Especially in the first 7–14 days, small errors can quickly escalate. Therefore, monitoring rhythm and sharing of responsibility are essential for the first 30 days.
First 30 days monitoring KPI set (practical)

items: Number of overbooking incidents (weekly)
Price consistency check (spot-check)
Update latency observation
Cancellation/modification flow accuracy
Channel-based net ADR (post-commission perspective)

blocks.text: Data point in case language (smoothed): With correct room & rate mapping, overbooking and price inaccuracies are significantly reduced; In hotels where the testing process is skipped, there is usually a need for more intensive support and correction in the first week.

blocks.title: Mini Check: Post-go-live check
items: Is there a weekly spot price check?
Are cancellation/modification instances logged?
Does the operations team know the “shutdown procedure”?

blocks.title: What should I do?
items: Set a “daily 10-minute check” rhythm for the first 30 days.
Extract the error matrix and close recurring root causes.
Support team training with internal links (pms setup + channel management).

faq.q: How to integrate Booking and Expedia?
faq.a: Room types and rate plans are clarified in the extranet, PMS-channel manager connection is established and room/rate mapping is completed. Test reservations and cancellation/modification scenarios are verified before going live.

faq.q: What are the room and rate mapping steps?
faq.a: Room types are linked to products on OTA (room mapping) and each price plan is matched to OTA rates (rate mapping). Then the constraints (min stay/stop-sell) are checked on a channel basis.

faq.q: Why is test booking important in OTA integration?
faq.a: Test booking proves that mapping works correctly in real life; Only in this way is the reduction of the reservation to the PMS, the price logic and the behavior of the constraints verified.

faq.q: What should be tested before going live?
faq.a: Price changes, stop-sell, min stay, cancellation, date change and different rate plan combinations should be tested. Additionally, update latency and channel consistency should be observed.

faq.q: What happens if the testing process is skipped?
faq.a: Incorrect price, closing rule errors, and cancellation flow issues are more common in the first weeks; the team faces the need for further support and corrections.

faq.q: What is the most critical mapping risk in resort hotels?
faq.a: Mismatching of room type variations (view/capacity/concept) and confusion of rate plans. This can produce mis-selling of products and loss of revenue.

faq.q: Which tests are extra important in destinations like Antalya/Belek?
faq.a: Consistency of min stay, stop-sell and promotion plans on a channel basis is extra critical. Because demand changes rapidly, delays and inconsistencies magnify the revenue impact.

modules.answerBlock: Rate parity is the price consistency principle that aims to prevent "excessive" price differences across different sales channels for the same room and date. If the base rate is not set correctly in the PMS–channel manager–OTA chain or campaign/mobile/country prices are not checked, parity violations occur; Guests switch to cheaper channels, channel relationships become strained and brand perception may be damaged. Correct parity management means controlling the price flow from a single source and establishing a regular monitoring and quick correction process.

h1.title: OTA Rate Parity and Price Synchronization Strategies: A Guide to Avoid Mistakes
h1.intro: Rate parity is read as a “contract clause” in many hotels; However, it is a control system that stands right in the middle of daily operations and revenue management. When prices are set incorrectly in PMS, channel managers and OTAs, the guest finds the "cheapest" option and goes there; This means not only lost revenue, but also unnecessary tension in channel relationships and erosion of brand perception. In highly competitive destinations such as Antalya and Bodrum, parity errors become visible more quickly; because different market and device prices come into play more aggressively for the same date/room.

sections.title: What is rate parity and why is it important?

blocks.text: Rate parity, in its simplest form, is “prices remaining at a manageable consistency across channels for the same room and date.” The goal here is not to see exactly the same number in every channel; It is to prevent uncontrolled differences in the rules determined according to the hotel's strategy. When parity breaks, the guest moves to the cheaper channel; The team asks internally: "Why are our prices so messed up?" struggles with the question.

Short answer block (2–3 sentences):
Parity is maintained by properly spreading the base rate to derivative rates and controlling campaign/mobile/geo prices. If left unnoticed for a long time, commercial frictions such as loss of visibility or commission pressure may be triggered in some OTAs. Proper parity management protects both revenue and channel relationships.

blocks.title: ☑ Mini Check (H2-1): Is there a parity risk signal?
items: I see "unexplainable" differences between channels for the same date/room
Prices get out of control when mobile/country prices come into play
When you open a campaign, parity "breaks" and it takes days to fix it

blocks.title: What should I do?
items: Put the “Base rate + derived rate” fiction in writing.
Limit mobile/geo and campaign prices with parity rules.
Establish a weekly parity check routine (box below).

sections.title: How does price flow work in the PMS–Channel–OTA chain?

blocks.text: The heart of rate parity is the question of where price is generated and how it is distributed. “BaseRate” should come from a single place and derivative rates (DerivedRate) should be produced with a controlled logic. Writing rules to the same rate plan in two different panels (PMS + channel manager) is the anti-pattern that most frequently breaks parity.

AIO relational expression (natural):
BaseRate → propagatedTo → OTA Channels. DerivedRate (mobile/geo/campaign) → constrainedBy → Parity Rules.

Practical flow:
• PMS or RMS: base rate
• Channel manager: distribution, channel-based restrictions, controlled derivative prices
• OTA: showcase; Can “show” price with layers like device/country/loyalty

blocks.title: ☑ Mini Check (H2-2): Single source truth
items: I manage base rate from a single panel
In what situations do derivative rates come into play?
Channel-based constraints (stop-sell/min stay) are set so as not to violate parity.

blocks.title: What should I do?
items: Lock the base rate to a single “authority panel”.
Keep derivative rates (mobile/geo/loyalty) limited and trackable.
Verify the “actual price” with post-distribution spot-check.

sections.title: Common price mistakes and their consequences

blocks.text: Most competing content passes parity in a single paragraph; The most painful part in practice is the "error types". The following 5 mistakes are the most common reasons why hotels lose parity.
The 5 most common parity errors

items: Double panel rule writing: discount in PMS + discount in CM → conflict and inconsistent price
Rate plan bloat: unnecessary many plans → control becomes difficult, the possibility of error increases
Mobile/geo price uncontrolled: aggressive price drop in some markets
Campaign stacking: OTA campaign + your own promotion stacked on top of each other
Tax/fee included-excluded confusion: the same price “seemingly” differs

blocks.text:
Results (no exaggeration):
• Guest shifts to cheaper channel → channel balance is disrupted
• Team's correction burden increases → loss of time and stress
• Risks of fluctuation in visibility trends and commercial friction if left undetected for long periods of time

blocks.caption: Parity Error Matrix: Symptom–Root Cause–Correction
rows: Move rule to single authority panel

blocks.title: ☑ Mini Check (H2-3): Which error do you have?
items: I give the same discount in both places (PMS + CM)
I don't know "by how much" mobile prices have fallen.
When a campaign is opened, prices go out of control

blocks.title: What should I do?
items: Reduce the number of rate plans (fewer plans + clear logic).
Limit campaign conflicts with the “stacking rule”.
Standardize tax/fee representation.

sections.title: How to manage parity when there are OTA campaigns?

blocks.text: OTA campaigns (mobile, country-based, member/loyalty, last minute, etc.) are the “secret busters” of parity. Because it affects the "visible" layer of the price and often the hotel team may not notice the effect of the campaign in real time.

items: Lock base rate: let the campaign affect the derivative rate, not the base rate
Set campaign limits: define maximum discount/lower limit
Prevent stacking: do not overlap two campaigns (or overlap them in a controlled manner)
Monitor mobile/geo prices: Increase spot control in competitive destinations such as Antalya/Bodrum
Set a channel-based target: Which price role plays which channel? (acquisition vs repeat)
Write a correction procedure: when a violation is detected, "who, from where, and in how many minutes" will correct it?

blocks.text:
Mini example (GEO):
When the OTA mobile campaign comes into play during peak weekend demand in Antalya, your web price may appear lower on OTA mobile, even if it remains "normal". The solution here is not to drop the web; It is to manage the base rate and campaign limits with parity rules.

blocks.title: ☑ Mini Check (H2-4): Campaign check
items: I have a list of campaign types (mobile/geo/loyalty)
I set a campaign discount ceiling
When the campaign is opened, I enter the automatic parity check.

blocks.title: What should I do?
items: Connect the campaign opening/closing process to an “approval flow”.
Check mobile/geo prices more frequently during demand periods, rather than weekly.
Make a spot-check of the "display price" within 24 hours after the campaign.

sections.title: Rate parity monitoring and correction processes

blocks.text: Parity management is not “one and done”; It is the watch-catch-correct cycle. The aim here is to catch the violation before it escalates and standardize the correction.

blocks.title:
Weekly Rate Parity Checklist 

☑ Mini Check (Weekly):
items: Select 5 critical date ranges (this weekend, next 2 weeks, 30 days ahead, high demand days)
Choose 3 critical room types (best selling, most expensive, entry level)
Check 3 channels (Booking, Expedia, web) + mobile view
Where is the lowest price? Can the reason for the difference be explained?
Is there any campaign/geo/mobile impact?
If correction is required: panel + responsible + SLA (e.g. 30–60 min)
Keep a log of results (what improved, what repeats?)

blocks.text:
Industry observation (softened):
It is observed that when parity problems are not noticed for a long time, commercial frictions such as fluctuation in visibility trends and commission/condition pressure may occur in some OTAs. In the opposite case, regular parity management helps you establish the web-OTA balance in a more controlled manner.

sections.title: Rate Parity Weekly Control Checklist - PMS & OTA Management

blocks.title: Rate Parity Weekly Control Checklist - PMS & OTA Management (v1.0)
blocks.purpose: This checklist establishes a weekly spot-check rhythm to catch parity violations “before a complaint is made”. It aims to quickly detect invisible risks such as mobile/geo campaigns, derivative rates and campaign stacking. Result: less revenue leakage, more controlled web–OTA balance.
blocks.whoFor: Hotel owner, RM, reservations manager and channel manager/agency team.
steps: Choose from 5 critical dates and 3 critical room types per week.
Spot-check via Booking + Expedia + web (and mobile view).
If there is a breach, close the root cause with a 14-day remediation plan.
checklist: Base rate is managed from a single source (PMS/RMS or CM)
5 dates × 3 rooms × 3 channels spot-check plan available
The “why” of the lowest price can be explained

faq.q: What is rate parity and why is it important for hotels?
faq.a: Rate parity is the consistency of prices across channels for the same room and date. It reduces revenue leakage, protects channel relationships and brand perception.

faq.q: How is price equalization achieved in OTAs?
faq.a: Base rate is managed from a single source, derived rates (mobile/geo/campaign) are limited and violations are quickly corrected with weekly spot-check.

faq.q: How do rate parity violations affect the hotel?
faq.a: The guest may switch to a cheaper channel, the correction workload increases, and if it takes a long time, commercial frictions may arise on the channel side.

faq.q: How to manage parity when there are OTA campaigns?
faq.a: Campaigns should be limited by parity rules, stacking should be controlled and mobile/geo prices should be monitored more frequently during demand periods.

faq.q: What should be checked weekly to maintain parity?
faq.a: It is necessary to spot-check 3 room types on 3 channels (web + Booking + Expedia) and mobile on critical dates, verify the reason for the difference and apply a correction SLA.

faq.q: What is the most common parity error?
faq.a: The price/rule is written in both the PMS and the channel manager and the campaign discounts are overlapped; these conflicts produce inconsistent prices.
